content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

June 21, 2016

Neal Reardon
6520 Tremont St. Unit B
Oakland, CA 94609

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-16-118

Dear Mr. Reardon:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the revolving door provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

Please note that we are only providing advice under the Act and not under other general
conflict of interest prohibitions such as the Public Contract Code or Section 1090.

QUESTIONS

1. What are the prohibitions imposed on you by the Act if you leave the Public Utilities
Commission (PUC) and work for a private sector employer (Sonoma Clean Power (SCP))?

2. May you attend public workshops and public Commission meetings of the PUC?

3. Once one year has passed after your departure from the PUC, may you meet with PUC
staff as necessary to discuss issues that impact SCP?

CONCLUSIONS

1. The Act does not prohibit you accepting employment with SCP but does restrict your
ability to appear before the PUC for one year and permanently prohibits you from participating in
any proceeding (such as any application, request for a ruling or other determination, contract, claim,
controversy, investigation, charge, accusation, arrest or other particular matter involving a specific
party or parties in any court or state administrative agency) that you participated in as a PUC
employee.

_________________________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-16-118
Page No. 2

2. You may attend public workshops and public Commission meetings of the PUC.

3. Once one year has passed after your departure from the PUC, you may meet with PUC staff as necessary to discuss issues that impact SCP so long as the meetings do not involve any "judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding" that you participated in as a PUC employee (as discussed below) since this would be prohibited by the permanent ban in Section 87401 and 87402.

FACTS²

You have been with the California Public Utilities Commission for seven years, working on energy issues. You served as an analyst for two programs and filed Form 700s in previous years.

• The distributed generation program is called the Self-Generation Incentive Program. It is funded by ratepayers and administered by the utilities. It provides customers with financial incentives to help them invest in clean technologies to reduce their onsite electrical demand. The proceeding number is R.12-11-005. The most active parties are companies that manufacture and/or install clean technologies (for example, SolarCity, Tesla, Bloom Energy).

For the Self-Generation Incentive Program, your duties have been to solicit comments from parties on program revisions, which included hosting a public workshop. You also met with the utility program administrators on a regular basis. Your work in the last three months was primarily summarizing party comments and contributing thoughts and data for a Proposed Decision which was recently issued. This Proposed Decision would revise the program rules, including streamlining the application process and requirements for on-going monitoring.

• The long-term procurement planning proceeding evaluates electric reliability and authorizes the utilities to issue requests for proposals when a need for more equipment is necessary. The proceeding number for the procurement planning proceeding that you worked on is R. 13-12-010. That proceeding is no longer active. The website link for the program is here: http://www.cpuc.ca.gov/General.aspx?id=6617

For the long-term procurement plan proceeding, you served as a project coordinator to ensure that the PUC received data in a timely manner from various sources to inform the proceeding. You also helped summarize party comments, mostly focused on whether the State needs more electrical generation to ensure reliability of the grid.

You have received an offer to work for a quasi-governmental non-profit organization designated as a Community Choice Aggregator called SCP. SCP is a small organization with just 14 employees. This group is not a party to either of the proceedings listed above. SCP offers alternative electric service to those provided by the local investor owned utility, with customers choosing which provider they want. This is their description of their organization from the website "is a not-for-profit agency, independently run by the Sonoma County cities that have joined the

² On June 10, 2016, you provided the following additional facts incorporated herein.

File No. A-16-118
Page No. 3

program, including Cloverdale, Cotati, Petaluma, Rohnert Park, Santa Rosa, Sebastopol, Sonoma,
and the Town of Windsor, as well as all of the unincorporated areas in the county."

Your title with SCP will be "Manager of Compliance and Regulatory Affairs" and you will
be tasked with monitoring laws and policies to 1) ensure that SCP is in compliance with all
requirements and, 2) to identify which proceedings are most likely to impact SCP and help develop
internal fact sheets to inform their opinions. The largest issue they are grappling with are the exit
fees that PG&E (the local utility) imposes on them every year.

As part of your duties with SCP, you plan to attend public workshops and public
Commission meetings of the PUC. You stated that you would not actively participate, but would
follow the conversation and take notes. At the end of the one-year ban you would plan to meet with
Commission staff as necessary to discuss issues that impact SCP.

ANALYSIS

Public officials who leave state service are subject to two types of post-governmental
employment provisions under the Act, the one-year ban and the permanent ban. In addition, Section
87407 and 87100 prohibits officials from making, participating in making, or using their position to
influence decisions affecting persons with whom they are negotiating employment, or have any
arrangement concerning employment. These provisions are commonly referred to as the "revolving
door" prohibitions.

One-Year Ban

The "one-year ban" prohibits a former state employee from making, for compensation, any
formal or informal appearance, or making any oral or written communication, before his or her
former agency for the purpose of influencing any administrative or legislative actions or any
discretionary act involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license,
grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property. (See Section 87406, Regulation
18746.1.)

The one-year ban applies to any employee of a state administrative agency who holds a
position that is designated or should be designated in the agency's conflict-of-interest code.
(Section 87406(d)(1); Regulation 18746.1(a)(2).) The ban applies for twelve months from the date
the employee permanently leaves state office or employment. While in effect, the one-year ban
applies only when a former employee or official is being compensated for his or her appearances or
communications before his or her former agency on behalf of any person as an agent, attorney, or
representative of that person. (Regulation 18746.1(b)(3) and (4).)

In contrast to the permanent ban, which only applies to certain matters involving specific
parties such as "judicial or quasi-judicial" proceedings, the one-year ban applies to "any appearance
or communication made for the purpose of influencing administrative or legislative action or
influencing any action or proceeding involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation
of a permit, license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property." (Regulation
18746.1(b)(5).)

File No. A-16-118
Page No. 4

An appearance or communication is for the "purpose of influencing" if it is made for the
"principal purpose of supporting, promoting, influencing, modifying, opposing, delaying, or
advancing the action or proceeding." (Regulation 18746.2.) An appearance or communication
includes, but is not limited to, conversing by telephone or in person, corresponding in writing or by
electronic transmission, attending a meeting, and delivering or sending any communication. (Ibid.)

An appearance or communication is not limited when an individual: (1) Participates as a
panelist or formal speaker at a conference or similar public event for educational purposes or to
disseminate research and the subject matter does not pertain to a specific action or proceeding; (2)
Attends a general informational meeting, seminar, or similar event; (3) Requests information
concerning any matter of public record; or (4) Communicates with the press. (Regulation
18746.2(b).)

Finally, appearances and communications are prohibited only if they are (1) before a state
agency that the public official worked for or represented, (2) before a state agency "which budget,
personnel, and other operations" are subject to the control of a state agency the public official
worked for or represented, or (3) before any state agency subject to the direction and control of the
Governor, if the official was a designated employee of the Governor's office during the twelve
months before leaving state office or employment. (Regulation 18746.1(b)(6).)

As an analyst at the PUC, you are subject to the one-year ban. Therefore, for one year after
leaving state service, you may not represent any person by appearing before or communicating with
the PUC or any PUC officer or employee to influence the issuance, amendment, awarding, or
revocation of a permit, license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property, or to
influence administrative or legislative action.

Permanent Ban

The "permanent ban" prohibits a former state employee from "switching sides" and
participating, for compensation, in certain proceedings involving the State of California and other
specific parties, or assisting in the proceeding if the proceeding is one in which the former state
employee participated while employed by the state. (Sections 87401 and 87402; Regulation
18741.1.) The permanent ban applies when an official has permanently left or takes a leave of
absence from any particular office or employment. (Regulation 18741.1(a)(1).)

The permanent ban is a lifetime ban and applies to any formal or informal appearance or any
oral or written communication – or aiding, advising, counseling, consulting, or assisting in
representing any other person, other than the State of California, in an appearance or
communication – made with the intent to influence any judicial, quasi-judicial, or other proceeding
in which you participated while you served as a state administrative official. "'Judicial, quasi-
judicial or other proceeding' means any proceeding, application, request for a ruling or other
determination, contract, claim, controversy, investigation, charge, accusation, arrest or other
particular matter involving a specific party or parties in any court or state administrative
agency . . .'" (Section 87400(c).)

Additionally, an official is considered to have "participated" in a proceeding if he or she
took part in the proceeding "personally, and substantially through decision, approval, disapproval,

File No. A-16-118
Page No. 5

formal written recommendation, rendering advice on a substantial basis, investigation, or use of
confidential information . . . ." (Section 87400(d).)

As an analyst employed by the PUC, you are a "state administrative official" for purposes of
the permanent ban. However, based on your description of your duties at the PUC it does not appear
that you were involved in any of the PUC's "proceedings" as contemplated by the permanent ban. If
this is the case, the prohibition would not apply.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Assistant General Counsel
Legal Division

JWW:jgl
